preamble: The transformation from the voiced postalveolar fricative /ʒ/ to the voiceless velar fricative /x/ is a notable historical sound change, particularly attested in the development of Castilian Spanish. This shift involves both a loss of voicing and a significant retraction of the place of articulation.

examples.note: In Old Spanish, the voiced postalveolar fricative /ʒ/ (represented by 'j' or 'g' before 'e', 'i') underwent devoicing to /ʃ/ and subsequently retracted to /x/ (the modern 'jota') around the mid-16th century.
The 'x' in place names like 'México' historically represented /ʃ/, which later shifted to /x/ in Castilian Spanish.

sources: How did French (and Portuguese) ended up with a [ʒ] and Spanish with a [x] when we all began with a J [ j ] + how did portuguese and french had the same change while spanish got a [x] (Romanian probably copied the French and Italian orthography or maybe not) : r - Reddit.
How did Spanish 'x' shift from a voiceless post-alveolar fricative, all the way to a voiceless velar fricative, in such a short time? Also, how did it get conflated with 'j'? - Quora.
What's the strangest sound shift you've ever seen in a language? : r/linguistics - Reddit.
